epigraph.text: For everything there is a season, and a time for every matter under heaven... a time to cast away stones, and a time to gather stones together.

content.text:
A transformation is a function that moves or changes a geometric figure 
in some way. In this chapter, we study four fundamental transformations: 
translations, reflections, rotations, and dilations. We begin with the 
simplest—the translation.

When you slide a book across a table without rotating it, you perform 
a translation. Every point of the book moves the same distance in the 
same direction. The book's shape, size, and orientation remain unchanged; 
only its position differs.

content.title: Translation
content.content:
A **translation** is a transformation that 
slides every point of a figure the same distance in the same direction. 
A translation is defined by a **translation vector** 
$\vec{v} = \langle a, b \rangle$, where $a$ represents the horizontal 
shift and $b$ represents the vertical shift.

content.text:
The notation $\langle a, b \rangle$ represents a vector in component form. 
Unlike a point $(a, b)$, a vector describes a direction and magnitude, 
not a location. We write vectors with angle brackets to distinguish 
them from points.

content.title: Image and Pre-image
content.content:
When a transformation is applied to a point $P$, the resulting point 
$P'$ is called the **image** of $P$. The original 
point $P$ is called the **pre-image**. We read 
$P'$ as "$P$ prime."

content.title: Translation Coordinate Rule
content.content:
A translation by vector $\vec{v} = \langle a, b \rangle$ maps any 
point $(x, y)$ to its image $(x', y')$ according to the rule:
\[
 (x, y) \to (x + a, y + b)
\]
We write this transformation as $T_{\langle a, b \rangle}(x, y) = (x + a, y + b)$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Translating a single point
content.problem: Translate the point $P(3, 5)$ by the vector $\vec{v} = \langle 4, -2 \rangle$.
content.solution:
Using the translation rule $(x, y) \to (x + a, y + b)$:
$$
 P'(x', y') &= (3 + 4, 5 + (-2)) \\
 &= (7, 3)
$$
The image of $P(3, 5)$ is $P'(7, 3)$.

content.title: Translating a point with negative components
content.problem: Translate the point $A(-2, 4)$ by the vector $\vec{v} = \langle -3, -5 \rangle$.

content.text:
To translate a polygon, we translate each of its vertices and then 
connect the image points. Since all points move by the same vector, 
the resulting figure is congruent to the original.

content.title: Translating a triangle
content.problem:
Translate $\triangle ABC$ with vertices $A(0, 0)$, $B(4, 0)$, and $C(2, 3)$ 
by the vector $\vec{v} = \langle 3, 2 \rangle$. Graph both triangles.

content.title: Translating a quadrilateral
content.problem:
Rectangle $PQRS$ has vertices $P(-3, 1)$, $Q(1, 1)$, $R(1, -2)$, and 
$S(-3, -2)$. Find the vertices of the image after translation by 
$\vec{v} = \langle 5, 4 \rangle$.

content.text:
Sometimes we know a point and its image, and we need to find the 
translation vector. We can work backward from the translation rule.

content.title: Finding a Translation Vector
content.content:
If point $P(x_1, y_1)$ is translated to image $P'(x_2, y_2)$, then 
the translation vector is:
\[
 \vec{v} = \langle x_2 - x_1, y_2 - y_1 \rangle
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Finding the translation vector
content.problem: Point $A(2, -1)$ is translated to $A'(7, 4)$. Find the translation vector.
content.solution:
Using the formula $\vec{v} = \langle x_2 - x_1, y_2 - y_1 \rangle$:
$$
 \vec{v} &= \langle 7 - 2, 4 - (-1) \rangle \\
 &= \langle 5, 5 \rangle
$$
The translation vector is $\langle 5, 5 \rangle$.

content.title: Verifying a translation
content.problem:
Triangle $ABC$ has vertices $A(1, 3)$, $B(4, 5)$, and $C(6, 1)$. 
Triangle $A'B'C'$ has vertices $A'(-2, 5)$, $B'(1, 7)$, and $C'(3, 3)$. 
Is $\triangle A'B'C'$ a translation of $\triangle ABC$? If so, find 
the translation vector.
content.solution:
Find the vector from each vertex to its image:
$$
 A \to A': \quad &\langle -2 - 1, 5 - 3 \rangle = \langle -3, 2 \rangle \\
 B \to B': \quad &\langle 1 - 4, 7 - 5 \rangle = \langle -3, 2 \rangle \\
 C \to C': \quad &\langle 3 - 6, 3 - 1 \rangle = \langle -3, 2 \rangle
$$
Since all three vectors are equal, $\triangle A'B'C'$ is a translation 
of $\triangle ABC$ by the vector $\langle -3, 2 \rangle$.

content.text:
Translations have several important properties that make them 
useful in geometry and applications.

content.title: Properties of Translations
content.content:
A translation has the following properties:

1. **Preserves distance:** The distance between any two 
 points equals the distance between their images.

2. **Preserves angle measure:** Angles are mapped to 
 congruent angles.

3. **Preserves orientation:** The order of vertices 
 (clockwise or counterclockwise) is unchanged.

4. **Maps lines to parallel lines:** A line and its 
 image under translation are parallel (or coincident).
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Isometry
content.content:
An **isometry** (or **rigid transformation**%
) is a transformation that preserves 
distance. Because isometries preserve distance, they also preserve 
angle measure and produce congruent figures. The image of a figure 
under an isometry is congruent to the original figure.

content.text:
Translations are isometries. When we translate a figure, the image 
is congruent to the pre-image.

content.title: Showing distance is preserved
content.problem:
Show that translation preserves distance for the segment from 
$A(1, 2)$ to $B(4, 6)$ translated by $\vec{v} = \langle 2, -3 \rangle$.
content.solution:
First, find the length of $\overline{AB}$:
\[
 AB = \sqrt{(4-1)^2 + (6-2)^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5
\]

Next, find the images:
$$
 A' &= (1 + 2, 2 - 3) = (3, -1) \\
 B' &= (4 + 2, 6 - 3) = (6, 3)
$$

Find the length of $\overline{A'B'}$:
\[
 A'B' = \sqrt{(6-3)^2 + (3-(-1))^2} = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5
\]

Since $AB = A'B' = 5$, the translation preserves distance.

content.title: Magnitude of a Vector
content.content:
The **magnitude** (or length) of 
a vector $\vec{v} = \langle a, b \rangle$ is denoted $|\vec{v}|$ or 
$\|\vec{v}\|$ and calculated as:
\[
 |\vec{v}| = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}
\]
The magnitude represents the distance that every point moves under 
the translation.

content.title: Finding vector magnitude
content.problem: Find the magnitude of the translation vector $\vec{v} = \langle -5, 12 \rangle$.
content.solution:
Using the magnitude formula:
\[
 |\vec{v}| = \sqrt{(-5)^2 + 12^2} = \sqrt{25 + 144} = \sqrt{169} = 13
\]
The magnitude is 13, meaning every point moves 13 units.

content.title: Real-world translation
content.problem:
A chess piece moves from square $(3, 2)$ to square $(5, 6)$ on a 
coordinate grid. Describe this move as a translation and find 
how far the piece traveled.
content.solution:
The translation vector is:
\[
 \vec{v} = \langle 5 - 3, 6 - 2 \rangle = \langle 2, 4 \rangle
\]

The piece moved 2 squares right and 4 squares up.

The distance traveled is:
\[
 |\vec{v}| = \sqrt{2^2 + 4^2} = \sqrt{4 + 16} = \sqrt{20} = 2\sqrt{5} \approx 4.47 \text{ units}
\]

content.text:
Translations appear throughout mathematics and the real world. 
In art and design, translations create repeating patterns. In 
physics, translations describe the motion of objects that move 
without rotating.

marginNotes.text:
Now the Lord said to Abram, "Go from 
your country and your kindred and your father's house to the land 
that I will show you."

The Lord will keep your going out and 
your coming in from this time forth and forevermore.

Behold, I am doing a new thing; now 
it springs forth, do you not perceive it? I will make a way in the 
wilderness and rivers in the desert.

Who went before you in the way 
to seek you out a place to pitch your tents, in fire by night and 
in the cloud by day.
